title: Occupation Dynamics of Floquet-Volkov States and Spectral Sum Rule
authors: Xuanxi Cai, Changhua Bao, Benshu Fan, Haoyuan Zhong, Fei Wang, Shaohua Zhou, Tianyun Lin, Hongyun Zhang, Pu Yu, Peizhe Tang, Wenhui Duan, Shuyun Zhou
abstract: Time-periodic light fields can dress electronic states in quantum materials, forming Floquet states whose dynamic occupation determines transient material properties. Here by using time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (TrARPES), we reveal the transient occupation of Floquet-Volkov states in two semiconductors, black phosphorus and MoSe$_2$. While the occupation of the light-induced sidebands, directly reflected by TrARPES spectral weight, strongly depends on the driving field, we find that the total spectral weight obtained by summing up all sidebands is conserved upon below-gap driving. Our work provides critical insights into the Floquet population dynamics, which are essential for light-field tailoring of transient material properties.
summary: This paper uses TrARPES to show that in semiconductors like black phosphorus, the total electron spectral weight is conserved when driving with below-gap mid-infrared light, despite the creation of multiple Floquet sidebands. This spectral sum rule is broken when the driving energy exceeds the bandgap, leading to direct interband transitions. These findings are crucial for controlling transient material properties via light-field engineering.
main_problem: Investigating the transient occupation dynamics of Floquet-Volkov states and determining the validity of a spectral sum rule in semiconductors under intense mid-infrared driving.
main_result: The study discovers that under below-gap driving, the total spectral weight of Floquet sidebands is conserved (a spectral sum rule), whereas this rule is violated during above-gap pumping due to interband transitions.
method: Time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (TrARPES) is used to measure spectral weight redistribution, complemented by Floquet theory and Bessel function approximations to model sideband intensities.
model_or_system: The experimental system consists of two semiconductors, black phosphorus and 2H-MoSe2, subjected to mid-infrared (160 meV) pulse driving.
key_observables: Floquet-Volkov sidebands, spectral weight S(k), isosbestic points in Energy Distribution Curves (EDCs), and pump fluence.
important_parameters: Below-gap vs. above-gap pumping regimes, pump photon energy (160 meV), and pump fluence (up to 1.1 mJ/cm2).
main_assumptions: The use of a Bessel function approximation neglects potential coupling with external baths, and the study does not cover the sub-cycle regime.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows spectral weight conservation in black phosphorus; Figure 3 shows conservation in MoSe2 at the K valley; Figure 4 compares the conservation in below-gap driving to the violation in above-gap driving.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Floquet-Volkov dynamics, presents TrARPES experimental results for black phosphorus and MoSe2, demonstrates the spectral sum rule and its violation, and provides a theoretical framework using Floquet theory and Bessel functions to explain the observed weight redistribution.
why_interesting: The discovery of a spectral sum rule and the observation of population inversion in light-dressed states provide fundamental insights into the non-equilibrium dynamics of driven quantum systems and the conservation laws governing energy-periodic state redistribution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Light-induced sidebands and spectral weight redistribution in black phosphorus upon below-gap pumping. (a) Schematic of TrARPES setup with MIR pumping. (b, c) Dispersion images measured at ∆t = −1 ps along the AC (b) and ZZ (c) directions. (d, e) Dispersion images measured at ∆t = 0 along the AC (d) and ZZ (e) directions. (f) EDCs at k = 0 for data in (c, e). The color-shaded areas are fitting peaks, which are used to extract the spectral weight for each individual peak. The pump has a photon energy of 160 meV and fluence of 0.5 mJ/cm2. The pump is p-polarized, with polarization along ZZ direction (ZZ-pump) for (d) and polarization along AC direction (AC-pump) for (e).

Figure 2: Observation of spectral weight conservation in black phosphorus. (a-e) Dispersion images measured along the direction at 30◦from the AC direction of black phosphorus with p-pol. at different delay times. The pump photon energy is 160 meV, and the pump fluence is 0.32 mJ/cm2. (f) Intensity of n = 1 sideband as a function of the delay time. (g) EDCs at k = 0 from data in (a-e), and fitting of the EDC at ∆t = 0 (bottom curve). (h) Extracted spectral weight for different sidebands at different delay times from EDCs in (g). The error bars are for the total spectral weight and are estimated via standard uncertainty propagation from the fitting parameters. (i) Calculated spectral weight...

Figure 3: Observation of spectral weight conservation in MoSe2. (a) Dispersion image of bulk MoSe2 measured along the Γ-K direction. The dashed box marks the band structure at the K valley, which is the main focus. (b-d) Dispersion images measured around the K valley without pump (b), with s-pol. (c) and p-pol. (d) pump. The pump photon energy is 311 meV and the pump fluence is 2.5 mJ/cm2. (e-g) Corresponding dispersions for data shown in (b-d). (h) EDCs at the K point for data shown in (b-d) and fitting results to extract the individual spectral weight. (i) Extracted spectral weight for different sidebands from EDC analysis in (h).

Figure 4: Conservation of spectral sum rule upon below-gap pumping, and violation of spec- tral sum rule upon above-gap pumping in black phosphorus. (a, b) A schematic illustration for the band structure and pump photon energies below the band gap Eg (a) and above the band gap (b). (c-f) Dispersion images measured along AC direction at ∆t = −1 ps (c) and at ∆t = 0 ps with three different pump photon energies (d-f). The pump is ZZ-pump (p-pol.) and the pump fluences are 0.29, 0.26 and 0.20 mJ/cm2, respectively. (g) Extracted spectral weight at the Γ point from data in (c-f).
